summary: Uncle Bob responds to a critique of TDD teaching methods by first refuting the argument that students should be taught to design up-front to avoid refactoring pain, then agreeing with it—but only when applied to architecture rather than domain design. He argues that learning to refactor is essential homework that cannot be shortcut, and that extraction and test maintenance are trivial when done correctly. However, he draws a crucial distinction: while TDD drives problem domain design through red-green-refactor, system architecture must be decided up-front because it exists in a different dimension entirely—the 'Domain Discontinuity.'
key_points: You learn complex things by solving problems, not by having solutions given to you—refactoring must be learned through experience
Tests should define behaviors, not mirror code structure; you don't rewrite tests when extracting methods and classes
Up-front design doesn't eliminate refactoring, it just postpones it until it becomes expensive
Architecture and problem domain are discontinuous—they differ in kind, not just level, and TDD cannot help with architectural decisions
System architecture is determined by desired user experience, not by problem domain, and must be decided before TDD can begin
money_quotes: You don't learn complex things by having the solutions given to you. You learn complex things by solving the problems that those complexities present.
I do not create a test for every method or every class. I create tests that define behaviors, and then I create the methods and classes that implement those behaviors.
We don't want the tests coupled to the code; and so we restrict the tests to operate through a small set of public methods.
Up front design does not eliminate the refactor step; it just postpones it until it is very expensive.
Architecture and problem domain are discontinuous – they do not form a continuum – they differ in kind not just in level.
TDD can't even be begun until we know the shape of the system that is to be created.
Like Shrodinger's cat, it lives in a superposition of states. It is right after all; except that it is dead wrong.
key_insight: TDD drives problem domain design through iterative refactoring, but system architecture exists in a separate dimension that must be decided up-front based on user experience—this Domain Discontinuity explains why the same advice can be simultaneously right and wrong.
